title: Observation of a tripartite quantum phase for coexisting extended, localized, and critical states
authors: Zhongshu Hu, Yajing Guo, Yu-Dong Wei, Bing-Chen Yao, Zhentian Qian, Xin-Chi Zhou, Bao-Zong Wang, Jianing Yang, Xuzong Chen, Shengjie Jin, Xiong-Jun Liu
abstract: The disordered quantum world hosts three fundamental types of states: extended, localized, and critical, of which the critical states are confined to fine-tuned critical points or mobility edges in randomly disordered systems. The tripartite phase, with all three types of states coexisting over finite spectral windows, represents a hallmark distinction between quasiperiodic and truly random systems in the localization physics. Here, we report the realization of this exotic phase in a quasi-periodically driven orbital optical lattice with ultracold atoms. The optical lattice with a quasiperiodic Floquet modulation coupling s and p orbitals is realized in experiment and shown to host the tripartite phase from exact theory. We develop a two-stage protocol to precisely prepare and detect the three types of quantum states. The characteristic exponents of these states are determined from expansion dynamics, showing their distinct universal transport properties. Our study marks a significant advancement in exploring unconventional critical phenomena and localization physics with ultracold atoms.
summary: This paper reports the experimental observation of a tripartite quantum phase where extended, localized, and critical states coexist in an orbital optical lattice. By using a two-stage Floquet protocol, the authors successfully prepare and distinguish these states using momentum-space and real-space diagnostics. The work demonstrates how inter-orbital coupling can be used to engineer complex localization physics.
main_problem: The observation and characterization of a tripartite quantum phase where extended, localized, and critical states coexist over finite spectral windows in a quasiperiodic system.
main_result: The researchers experimentally realized and identified a tripartite phase in an orbital optical lattice, demonstrating that inter-orbital coupling creates incommensurately distributed zeros that drive the emergence of critical states.
method: The study uses a two-stage Floquet engineering protocol (frequency and amplitude ramps) for state preparation, combined with Renormalization Group (RG) analysis for theoretical validation and expansion dynamics for transport characterization.
model_or_system: Ultracold 87Rb atoms in a 1D quasiperiodically driven s-p orbital optical lattice, where s and p orbitals are coupled via Floquet modulation.
key_observables: Momentum distributions (via TOF), Fractal Dimension (FD), Spatial Coherence Length (L_Coh), Momentum-space Correlation Length (k_Cor), and expansion exponents (alpha).
important_parameters: Inter-orbital coupling (Omega), quasiperiodic modulation strength (delta_s, delta_p), shaking frequency (f), and shaking amplitude (A).
main_assumptions: The system is treated as non-interacting (mean-field interactions are negligible), and the primary lattice is deep enough to justify a localized Wannier basis.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Experimental setup and visual comparison of wavefunctions; Fig 2: Fractal dimension and phase regions; Fig 3: State preparation pathways; Fig 5: Phase diagram via dual-space analysis; Fig 7: Momentum distribution micromotion; Fig 8: Expansion dynamics.
paper_structure: Introduction to the tripartite phase problem, experimental setup and Floquet engineering description, development of the two-stage preparation protocol, identification of the three phases using dual-space correlation lengths, verification via expansion dynamics and RG theory, and discussion of micromotion and robustness.
why_interesting: It provides a versatile platform for studying unconventional critical phenomena, localization physics, and the transition between different types of quantum states using Floquet engineering.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Schematic of the incommensurately driven s-p orbital lattice and characteristic states. a, Experimental configuration. A Bose-Einstein condensate is loaded into a one-dimensional quasiperiodic optical lattice (along the x direction) generated by superimposing a primary (λ0 = 1064 nm) and a secondary (λ1 = 760 nm) lattice. Absorption imaging is performed along the z direction. The relative displacement of the lattices is controlled by modulating the driving frequency of the acoustic-optic modulator (AOM) for the secondary lattice. A levitation beam, shaped as a sheet along the x direction, provides a strong vertical gradient force to counteract gravity along the z direction, while...

Figure 2. Two-stage protocol for selective state preparation. a,b, Fractal dimensions (FDs) of all eigenstates as functions of the detuning ∆ (a) and coupling strength Ω(b), serving as schematic representations of the evolution of quantum states. The color scale denotes the FD, where Path 1 (black) and Path 2 (gray) represent the distinct evolutionary trajectories. Stage 1 is executed in (a) via an adiabatic frequency sweep (solid lines) to bridge the s-p orbital gap, while Stage 2 is performed in (b) through an amplitude ramp (dashed lines). In these processes, the initial localized state L evolves into intermediate extended states E1 and E2, respectively. Subsequently, E1 adiabatically...

Figure 3. Quantitative identification of the tripartite phase via characteristic lengths. a, Schematic illustration of the pathway dependence of the final states. The upper panel shows the trajectory in the (A, f) parameter space, and the lower panel depicts the corresponding state evolution ψ(t). All paths terminate at the same final Hamiltonian (Af, ff) (upper), with different choices of (Ai, fi) rendering different types of target high energy states (lower). b,c, Spatial coherence length LCoh from experiment (b) and simulation (c) as a function of the initial shaking frequency fi and amplitude Ai. LCoh quantifies spatial extent; its suppression in region I identifies the localized...

Figure 4. Expansion dynamics. a, Bimodal Gaussian fit of the expanded atomic cloud. The upper panel shows the in-situ absorption image, while the lower panels present Gaussian fits to the corresponding one-dimensional density profiles. In the localized regime, the atomic cloud remains nearly stationary after sudden trap release and a 15 ms hold; a single Gaussian fit yields the cloud radius. In extended and critical regimes, significant expansion leads to a bimodal distribution. A bimodal Gaussian fit then determines the radii of both the expanding and localized components. b, Time evolution of the radii of three types of states.
